name: sca_indexer_down
description: SCA with indexer down — the most critical SCA manager scenario. Combines SCA FULL sync burst with an unavailable indexer.
notes: CRITICAL SCENARIO: This is the SCA dual-queue saturation case (§8.4 MS2).
Chain of events when indexer goes down:
1. WorkersQueue keeps receiving DataValues from 300 agents (UNLIMITED).
2. Workers process messages: write to RocksDB, push to IndexerQueue.
3. IndexerQueue's single worker tries bulkIndex → indexer unreachable.
4. IndexerQueue returns Status_Offline on StartAck for NEW sessions.
5. But EXISTING sessions' data already written to RocksDB stays there.
6. Meanwhile: ModuleCheck sessions enter retry loop (5x10s = 50s blocking).
Memory accumulation (300 agents x 500 checks x 4 KB avg):
- WorkersQueue: up to 600 MB if enqueue > dequeue rate.
- RocksDB disk: 300 x 500 x 4 KB = 600 MB in queue/inventory_sync/.
- IndexerQueue: 300 Response{Context} objects waiting to send to indexer.
SCA-specific dimension: After indexer comes back, the manager must process
the ChecksumModule verification — calling calculateChecksumOfChecksums()
which does paginated queries to the indexer. If the indexer just recovered
and those docs haven't been indexed yet, checksum mismatches trigger the
agent to do performRecovery() → FULL sync again → amplification loop.
This is the most dangerous SCA production scenario: indexer maintenance
window + agent fleet restart = double-queue saturation + disk exhaustion.
Compare with sca_burst: same message pattern but with the indexer sink blocked.
Proxy: RSS should show a sustained positive slope (not sawtooth) during downtime.
If RSS doesn't drop after indexer restart → queues don't drain → leak confirmed.
